content_html:
MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 9 – “P” for PricePart 1 - Major pricing strategies
Chapter 9 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing,
8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

9.1 Identify the three main pricing strategies, and discuss the importance of understanding customer-value perceptions, company costs and competitor strategies when setting prices.
9.2 Identify and define the other important external and internal factors affecting a firm’s pricing decisions.
9.3 Describe the main strategies for pricing new products.
Learning Objectives for Lecture 9 (1 of 2)

9.4 Explain how companies determine a set of prices that maximises the profits from the total product mix.
9.5 Discuss how companies adjust their prices to take into account different types of customers and situations.
9.6 Discuss the key issues related to initiating and responding to price changes.
Learning Objectives for Lecture 9 (2 of 2)

Part 1  - Major pricing strategies

Price is the amount of money charged for a product or service, or the sum of the values consumers exchange for the benefits of having or using the product or service.
Price :
is the only element in the marketing mix that produces revenue; all other elements represent costs
is one of the most flexible marketing mix elements
is also the number-one problem facing many marketing executives
has a direct impact on a firm’s bottom line.
What is a price?

Major pricing strategies (1 of 11)
The price the company charges will fall somewhere between one that is too high to produce any demand and one that is too low to produce a profit.
Here are some of the considerations in setting price:
customer perception
costs
profits
competition.
Q: What are some other considerations in setting price?

Figure 9.1
Considerations in setting price

Major pricing strategies (3 of 11)
There are three main pricing strategies:
customer value-based pricing
cost-based pricing
competition-based pricing.

Figure 9.2
Value-based pricing versus cost-based pricing

Major pricing strategies (5 of 11)
Customer value-based pricing
Customer value-based pricing refers to the process of setting the price based on buyers’ perceptions of value, rather than on the seller’s costs.
Good-value pricing
Good-value pricing refers to the process of offering just the right combination of quality and good service that customers want at a fair price.
Q: Are ‘good value’ and ‘low price’ the same thing?

Major pricing strategies (6 of 11)
Customer value-based pricing (cont.)
Value-added pricing
Value-added pricing refers to the process where marketers, rather than cutting prices to match competitors’ prices, attach value-added features and services to differentiate their offerings; and this supports higher prices.

Major pricing strategies (7 of 11)
Cost-based pricing
Cost-based pricing refers to the process of setting prices based on the costs for producing, distributing and selling the product, plus a fair rate of return for company’s effort and risk.

Major pricing strategies (8 of 11)
Cost-based pricing (cont.)
Types of costs
Fixed costs (overhead) are those costs that do not vary with production or sales level.
Variable costs are those costs that vary directly with the level of production.
Total costs are the sum of the fixed costs and variable costs for any given level of production.
Q: Consider a restaurant meal you have purchased recently. What were the restaurant’s variable and fixed costs for your meal?

Major pricing strategies (9 of 11)
Cost-based pricing (cont.)
Cost-plus pricing
Cost-plus pricing (markup pricing) refers to the process of adding a standard markup to the cost of the product.
Breakeven pricing
Breakeven pricing (target-return pricing) refers to the process of setting the price to break even on the costs of making and marketing a product or to make the desired profit.

Figure 9.3
Breakeven chart for determining target-return price and breakeven volume

Major pricing strategies (11 of 11)
Competition-based pricing
Competition-based pricing refers to the process of setting prices based on competitors’ strategies, costs, prices and market offerings.
Consumers base their judgments of a product’s value on the prices that competitors charge for similar products.
Q: ‘The goal is not to match or beat competitors’ prices.’ Do you agree with this statement? Why, or why not?

Major pricing strategies
A: 1. Likely reactions of competitors: if we lower our price will competitors also lower their prices? This could lead to a price war where we and the competitors all end up with low or no profits.
2. Likely reactions of competitors: If we raise our price will competitors “follow our lead” and increase their prices too? Would lead to same market shares for all competitors but with higher profits for all.
3. What is our aim profit level?
4. Need to harmonize the price of this item with other products in our product line (range).
5. Impact on our brand image and positioning: if we raise price, it might re-position the brand upwards, if we lower the price will this make our brand look down-market?
Q: What are some other considerations in setting price?

Major pricing strategies
A: They are not the same. “Low price” means what it says – that the price is low. “Good value” means that the price being charged is fair for the bundle of benefits that the product provides to the customer, which could be that the price is high but the benefits are also high.
A: Fixed cost would be rent, most of the costs of gas, electricity and water, and the cost of wages of the minimum number of staff required for the restaurant to operate. Variable costs would be the costs of extra staff needed to cope with workloads beyond what can be done by the minimum number of staff, plus the cost of ingredients per dish served and drinks sold, plus extra consumption of utilities.
Q: Consider a restaurant meal you have purchased recently. What were the restaurant’s variable and fixed costs for your meal?
Q: Are ‘good value’ and ‘low price’ the same thing?

Major pricing strategies
A: The goal is to achieve the agreed pricing objectives, which are to achieve one of:
Target profit amount or Return on Investment (RoI), ,
Target sales volume,
Target market share.
Q: ‘The goal is not to match or beat competitors’ prices.’ Do you agree with this statement? Why, or why not?

Coming up next
Part 2 - Other internal and external considerations affecting price decisions
